title: You Are What You (Don’t) Eat
gist: Historian James McWilliams uses an Austin couple's dramatic vegan-diet reversal of diabetes and obesity to argue that American 'cult diets' (vegan, paleo, primal, etc.) function as quasi-religious identity movements — per psychologist Kima Cargill's research — that provide motivation and community but, echoing Mark Lilla's critique of identity politics, fragment the cross-partisan coalition needed to actually reform an industrial food system that has grown ever more homogenized (just 5 animals and 12 plants supply 75% of world food) even as cult diets have proliferated.

themes.theme: A case study in extreme dietary intervention

insights.claim: Nearly 40% of the US adult population is obese, per a 2015-2016 CDC data brief, framing morbid obesity and diabetes as the default outcome of the standard American diet of salt, starch, and sweetness that the food industry both offers and actively encourages.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: Establishes the scale of the metabolic-disease baseline (~40% of US adults obese) against which any food-system or dietary-intervention narrative should be measured.
insights.locator: As nearly 40 percent of the adult US population can attest

insights.claim: James and Becca Reed, a lower-income Austin, Texas couple on 32 combined medications for morbid obesity, diabetes, and depression, reversed their diabetes within three months of going vegan: Becca's blood sugar fell from 200 mg/dL to 80 mg/dL and her A1C dropped from 9.75 to 5.8 (below the 6.5 diabetic threshold), James's cholesterol fell from over 200 to 153, and both lost 15-20 pounds a month while discontinuing most medications.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: A documented, quantified extreme-outcome case (diabetes reversal, major weight loss, medication discontinuation in three months) that dietary/health-outcomes analysts can use as a real-world reference point.
insights.quote: Becca’s blood sugar level dropped from an alarming 200 milligrams per deciliter (mg/dL) to a very normal 80 mg/dL
insights.locator: The miraculous part is in the numbers

insights.claim: Psychologist Kima Cargill's thesis in Food Cults is that 'membership in food cults serves the same psychological functions of cult membership of any kind,' with diets like veganism, paleo, and primal functioning to delineate in-group and out-group identity rather than primarily to optimize health.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: Reframes the entire diet/wellness market as an identity-and-belonging product category first and a nutrition product category second — relevant to how health/wellness brands should actually be positioned and evaluated.
insights.quote: membership in food cults serves the same psychological functions of cult membership of any kind.

insights.claim: Cargill argues that secularization has left a spiritual void that 'food cults arguably replace what religion once did by prescribing organized food rules and rituals,' explaining why dietary identities like veganism have taken on quasi-religious intensity in America.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: Positions the modern diet-culture boom as a downstream effect of religious decline — a structural tailwind for identity-driven food brands (and a reason such trends may prove durable rather than faddish).
insights.quote: food cults arguably replace what religion once did by prescribing organized food rules and rituals.

insights.claim: McWilliams argues there is nothing inherently healthy about veganism itself ('it's possible, indeed easy, to be an unhealthy vegan'); the Reeds' actual recovery came from unremarkable practices — more exercise, portion control, and eating real food instead of processed junk — with the vegan identity providing the motivational and community structure needed to sustain those changes.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: A key discipline for evaluating diet-brand health claims: the mechanism of benefit is often behavioral adherence and community support, not the specific diet's inherent nutritional superiority.
insights.quote: It’s possible, indeed easy, to be an unhealthy vegan.
insights.locator: As a physical matter, the Reeds did not regain their health

themes.theme: Identity politics undermines a unified food-reform push

insights.claim: McWilliams applies Mark Lilla's The Once and Future Liberal critique of identity-focused progressive politics to dietary culture, arguing the proliferation of competing 'cult diets' (vegan, paleo, primal, Mediterranean, etc.) fragments what could otherwise be a cross-partisan push for food-system reform, since both libertarians and Marxists at a farmers' market want a trustworthy, healthful food supply.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: Reframes food-system reform as a potentially bipartisan-addressable political opportunity currently squandered by dietary identity fragmentation — relevant to how food-policy coalitions could actually be built.
insights.quote: Go to any farmers’ market and you are as likely to bump into a libertarian as a Marxist, both of whom want to know their farmer and the quality of the food he grows.
insights.locator: his critique of liberalism as a movement fragmented by identity politics

insights.claim: McWilliams observes (citing Cargill's research on paleo dieters) that diet-cult adherents police the 'purity' of insiders far more intensely than they engage the 'meat-eating masses' outside the group, comparing this to campus activists policing each other's language while ignoring how the world outside the university actually speaks.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: Explains why diet-identity movements rarely convert outsiders at scale — their social energy is spent on internal purity enforcement rather than external persuasion, a pattern with parallels in other identity-based political movements.
insights.quote: will vegans exercise extreme vigilance over the converted rather than worry too much about the meat-eating masses.

insights.claim: Big agriculture's core problems — emphasis on factory-farmed meat and dairy, fertilizer-intensive corn and soy production, and the failure to grow a diversity of nutrient-dense plants for direct human consumption rather than animal feed — have become more entrenched over the past half-century even as cult diets have proliferated in parallel.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: Argues that individual dietary choices have run in parallel with, not against, industrial agriculture's consolidation — a bearish signal for the idea that consumer 'voting with forks' is meaningfully reforming the underlying supply chain.
insights.quote: the emphasis on factory-farmed meat and dairy, fertilizer-intensive corn and soy production, the failure to grow a diversity of nutrient-dense plants for people to eat

insights.claim: Just five animal species and twelve plant species supply 75% of the world's food, and just three plants (corn, rice, wheat) supply nearly 60% of humanity's plant-based calories, despite an estimated 50,000 edible plant species in existence, per Michael Pollan's The Omnivore's Dilemma.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: Quantifies the extreme narrowness of the global food supply chain relative to biological potential — a structural data point for agtech, alt-protein, and crop-diversity investment theses.
insights.quote: Today, 75 percent of the world’s food derives from just five animals and twelve plants.
insights.locator: But a deeper answer might consider the irony

insights.claim: Chicken displaced beef as the most-consumed meat in the United States, with each American eating 48.8 kg of chicken in 2017 — evidence the food system is narrowing toward a single dominant protein rather than diversifying.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: A concrete consumption-share data point (48.8 kg per capita, 2017) relevant to protein-market sizing and the trajectory of US meat consumption away from beef and toward poultry.
insights.quote: each American ate 48.8 kg of chicken per person in 2017, replacing beef as the most consumed meat in the country.

insights.claim: McWilliams's central thesis is that individual dietary conversion ('voting with forks') is 'a cop-out' that distracts from the harder, collective project of reimagining a radically diverse, accessible, nutrient-dense, and animal-welfare-respecting food system, since there is no reason to assume the industrial food system got things right on its first attempt.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: The article's bottom-line policy prescription: treats personal dietary choice as an insufficient lever for food-system change, implying that durable food-system reform requires collective/structural action rather than consumer-driven market signals alone.
insights.quote: a diet is the last place you would start.
insights.locator: Given the radical homogenization of the food system

insights.claim: McWilliams frames the industrial diet as a very recent 'thunderbolt' in human history — humans have eaten food for almost three million years and practiced agriculture for about twelve thousand, but the current industrial food system and its health consequences emerged only in roughly the last fifty years.
insights.speaker: James McWilliams
insights.so_what: Implies the current obesity/diet crisis is a recent, reversible engineering failure of a young system rather than an inevitable feature of modern civilization — a framing that supports optimism about redesigning the food system rather than merely adapting to it.
insights.quote: human beings have been eating food for almost three million years, but have been practicing agriculture for only about the last twelve thousand.
